title: Oxidation and Reduction
subtitle: Chapter 6: Redox Reactions — Complete NEB Notes

content.text: 1. Introduction to Oxidation and Reduction
Oxidation and reduction are complementary processes that occur simultaneously in chemical reactions. These reactions are called Redox Reactions.
2. Classical Concept of Oxidation
Oxidation: The process that involves addition of oxygen or any electronegative element, or removal of hydrogen or any electropositive element.

Examples: • C + O₂ → CO₂ (Addition of oxygen) • Mg + Cl₂ → MgCl₂ (Addition of electronegative element) • H₂S + Cl₂ → 2HCl + S (Removal of hydrogen) • 2KI + Cl₂ → 2KCl + I₂ (Removal of electropositive element K)
3. Classical Concept of Reduction
Reduction: The process that involves removal of oxygen or any electronegative element, or addition of hydrogen or any electropositive element.

Examples: • CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O (Removal of oxygen) • FeCl₃ + ½H₂ → FeCl₂ + HCl (Removal of electronegative element Cl) • Cl₂ + H₂ → 2HCl (Addition of hydrogen) • 2HgCl₂ + SnCl₂ → Hg₂Cl₂ + SnCl₄ (Addition of electropositive element)
4. Modern Electronic Concept
Oxidation: The process that involves loss of electrons. Simply, loss of electrons is called oxidation.
Examples: • Na → Na⁺ + e • Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻ • Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ + e⁻ • 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻
Reduction: The process that involves gain of electrons. Gain of electrons is called reduction.
Examples: • Cl₂ + 2e⁻ → 2Cl⁻ • O₂ + 4e⁻ → 2O²⁻ • Fe³⁺ + e⁻ → Fe²⁺ • Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu

content.label: 💡 Memory Trick
content.text: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons) • LEO says GER: Lose Electrons Oxidation, Gain Electrons Reduction
5. Oxidizing and Reducing Agents
Oxidizing Agent (Oxidant): A substance that oxidizes another substance but itself gets reduced in the reaction. It accepts electrons.
Examples: All non-metals are oxidizing agents (except H and noble gases). Acids like H₂SO₄, HNO₃ are oxidizing agents. Substances like MnO₂, KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇ are strong oxidizing agents.
Reducing Agent (Reductant): A substance that reduces another substance but itself gets oxidized in the reaction. It donates electrons.
Examples: All metals are reducing agents. Non-metals like C, H₂, S are reducing agents. Compounds like H₂S, SO₂, HI are reducing agents.
6. Oxidation Number (Oxidation State)
Oxidation Number: The residual charge that an atom appears to have when all other atoms from the molecule are removed as ions with their bonding electrons assigned to the more electronegative atom.
6.1 Rules for Assigning Oxidation Number
Rule 1: Oxidation number of an element in its free or elementary state is zero. Example: H₂ = 0, O₂ = 0, Na = 0, Cl₂ = 0
Rule 2: Oxidation number of elements in Group IA (alkali metals) is +1 and Group IIA (alkaline earth metals) is +2 in their compounds.
Rule 3: Oxidation number of hydrogen is +1 in most compounds (except in metal hydrides where it is -1).
Rule 4: Oxidation number of oxygen is -2 in most compounds (except in peroxides where it is -1, and in OF₂ where it is +2).
Rule 5: Sum of oxidation numbers of all atoms in a neutral molecule is equal to zero.
Rule 6: Sum of oxidation numbers of all atoms in an ion is equal to the ionic charge.

content.label: Quick Reference
content.text: F is always -1 • Cl, Br, I are -1 except with O or F • Alkali metals always +1 • Alkaline earth metals always +2 • Al is always +3
7. Calculating Oxidation Numbers - Examples
Example 1: Calculate oxidation number of S in H₂SO₄

content.name: Solution
content.note: Oxidation number of S in H₂SO₄ is +6

content.text: Example 2: Calculate oxidation number of Cr in K₂Cr₂O₇

content.name: Solution
content.note: Oxidation number of Cr in K₂Cr₂O₇ is +6

content.text: Example 3: Calculate oxidation number of Mn in KMnO₄

content.name: Solution
content.note: Oxidation number of Mn in KMnO₄ is +7

content.text: Example 4: Calculate oxidation number of N in NO₃⁻

content.name: Solution
content.note: Oxidation number of N in NO₃⁻ is +5

content.text: 8. Balancing Redox Reactions - Oxidation Number Method
Steps for Balancing:
1. Write the unbalanced chemical equation and assign oxidation numbers to all atoms
2. Identify the elements that undergo change in oxidation number (oxidation and reduction)
3. Calculate the increase and decrease in oxidation number
4. Make the increase equal to decrease by multiplying with suitable coefficients
5. Balance all atoms except H and O
6. Balance O atoms by adding H₂O
7. Balance H atoms by adding H⁺ (in acidic medium) or OH⁻ (in basic medium)
9. Solved Examples - Balancing Redox Reactions
Example 1: Balance: Cu + HNO₃ → Cu(NO₃)₂ + NO + H₂O

content.name: Solution
content.formula: \text{Step 1: Assign oxidation numbers} \\ Cu^0 + H\overset{+5}{N}O_3 \rightarrow Cu^{+2}(NO_3)_2 + \overset{+2}{N}O + H_2O \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Identify changes} \\ Cu: 0 \rightarrow +2 \quad \text{(increase by 2)} \\ N: +5 \rightarrow +2 \quad \text{(decrease by 3)} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Equalize changes (multiply Cu by 3, N by 2)} \\ 3Cu + 8HNO_3 \rightarrow 3Cu(NO_3)_2 + 2NO + 4H_2O

content.text: Example 2: Balance: KMnO₄ + HCl → KCl + MnCl₂ + Cl₂ + H₂O

content.name: Solution
content.formula: \text{Oxidation number changes:} \\ Mn: +7 \rightarrow +2 \quad \text{(decrease by 5)} \\ Cl: -1 \rightarrow 0 \quad \text{(increase by 1, but 2 Cl atoms)} \\ \\ \text{Multiply KMnO₄ by 2 and Cl₂ by 5} \\ 2KMnO_4 + 16HCl \rightarrow 2KCl + 2MnCl_2 + 5Cl_2 + 8H_2O

content.text: Example 3: Balance: K₂Cr₂O₇ + FeSO₄ + H₂SO₄ → K₂SO₄ + Cr₂(SO₄)₃ + Fe₂(SO₄)₃ + H₂O

content.name: Solution
content.formula: \text{Oxidation number changes:} \\ Cr: +6 \rightarrow +3 \quad \text{(decrease by 3, but 2 Cr atoms = 6)} \\ Fe: +2 \rightarrow +3 \quad \text{(increase by 1, but 2 Fe atoms = 2)} \\ \\ \text{Multiply FeSO₄ by 6 and K₂Cr₂O₇ by 1} \\ K_2Cr_2O_7 + 6FeSO_4 + 7H_2SO_4 \rightarrow K_2SO_4 + Cr_2(SO_4)_3 + 3Fe_2(SO_4)_3 + 7H_2O

content.text: 10. More Practice Examples
Example 4: Zn + HNO₃ → Zn(NO₃)₂ + NH₄NO₃ + H₂O
Example 5: Cu + HNO₃ → Cu(NO₃)₂ + NO₂ + H₂O
Example 6: I₂ + NaOH → NaI + NaIO₃ + H₂O (Disproportionation)

content.name: Balanced Equation
content.note: I₂ undergoes both oxidation (0→+5) and reduction (0→-1)

content.text: 11. Important Formulas Summary

content.name: Oxidation Number Rules
content.formula: \sum \text{O.N. in neutral molecule} = 0 \\ \sum \text{O.N. in ion} = \text{ionic charge}

content.name: Redox Balancing
content.formula: \text{Total increase in O.N.} = \text{Total decrease in O.N.}

content.label: 🎓 Exam Tips
content.text: • Oxidation and reduction always occur together • Oxidizing agent gets reduced, reducing agent gets oxidized • In peroxides (H₂O₂, Na₂O₂), O has O.N. = -1 • In metal hydrides (NaH, CaH₂), H has O.N. = -1 • F always has O.N. = -1 (most electronegative) • For balancing, first balance atoms other than H and O • Then balance O by adding H₂O • Finally balance H by adding H⁺ or OH⁻
12. Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Forgetting that oxidation number is different from actual charge • ❌ Not considering all atoms when calculating total change • ❌ Mixing up oxidizing agent with oxidation • ❌ Forgetting to multiply by number of atoms in molecule • ❌ Not balancing charge in ionic equations • ❌ Assuming O is always -2 (remember peroxides and OF₂) • ❌ Assuming H is always +1 (remember metal hydrides)
